scenarioChecks.label: Positive activation evidence does not recommend pivot or pause

source.assumption: The buyer values speed from idea to action more than perfect market research depth.
source.decision: Keep weekly activation-fix recommendations in the MVP and defer predictive churn scoring.
source.nextAction: Test whether founders will upload support notes before connecting a help-desk integration.

evidence.note: Four founders preferred a weekly prioritized fix list over another analytics dashboard.
Three founders identified a real onboarding change within ten minutes.

brief.headline: Weekly activation-fix recommendations are supported by founder preference, but evidence base is small and short on depth metrics.

claims.text: Founders preferred a weekly prioritized fix list over an additional analytics dashboard.
During a prototype walkthrough, founders could identify a real onboarding change within ten minutes, indicating low time-to-action.
Only five founders were interviewed and no quantitative depth-vs-speed tradeoff data was collected, so the assumption is directionally but not yet strongly supported.

unresolvedRisks: Sample size is limited to five founders, raising generalizability concerns.
No direct comparison measured time-to-action against depth of market research insight.
Founders were observed with the team's own prototype, which may bias positive feedback.
Whether founders will upload support notes before integrating a help-desk tool is untested and could break the recommended-fix workflow.
nextActions: Run a small A/B test exposing some founders to a research-depth-heavy view to quantify the speed-vs-depth tradeoff before full commitment.
Recruit at least 10-15 additional founders outside the initial five to validate preference patterns.
Test the unvalidated workflow: can founders reliably upload support notes before connecting a help-desk integration?
Add an instrumentation hook to measure time-to-first-actionable-fix as a leading indicator for the assumption.

scenarioChecks.label: Neutral clinic evidence does not overclaim proceed
Clinic brief keeps weak evidence marked insufficient

source.assumption: The buyer values speed from idea to action more than perfect market research depth.
source.nextAction: Run two privacy-reviewed shadow sessions with clinic administrators.

evidence.note: Administrators valued draft assistance but required explicit human approval before any schedule change.

brief.headline: Speed-over-depth preference is plausible but unconfirmed; current shadow evidence is neutral and limited

claims.text: Clinic administrators valued draft assistance but required explicit human approval before any schedule change, which is a neutral signal on whether they value speed over research depth.

unresolvedRisks: Only a single neutral evidence point exists; no direct signal on speed-vs-depth trade-off from buyers.
Sample size of one shadowing session is too small to generalize about administrator preferences.
Administrators' human-approval requirement may slow execution and partially contradict a speed-first stance.
nextActions: Run the two planned privacy-reviewed shadow sessions with clinic administrators to gather direct signal on speed vs. depth preference.
Include explicit questions or scenarios comparing fast AI-assisted drafts against deeper, slower market-research-backed options.
Capture approval latency and override rates as quantitative proxies for speed tolerance.

scenarioChecks.label: Challenge-only creator evidence does not recommend proceed
Creator challenge is represented in grounded claims

source.assumption: The buyer values speed from idea to action more than perfect market research depth.
source.decision: Drop automated campaign generation from the first release and focus on offer validation.
source.nextAction: Test three offer-positioning variants with a ten-creator waitlist.

evidence.note: Creators clicked the offer-validation message twice as often as the automated-campaign message.

brief.headline: Speed-to-action assumption is refuted; redirect first release toward offer validation.

claims.text: Creators preferred the offer-validation message over the automated-campaign message on the landing page, directly challenging the speed-over-research assumption.
The single available evidence point is a landing-page message test, which is suggestive of preference but not a validated measure of willingness to pay or activation behavior.

unresolvedRisks: Only one data source and one signal type; no activation, retention, or revenue evidence yet.
Landing-page click preference may not translate to actual product usage of an offer-validation workflow.
Confidence in the refutation is medium, not high, so reversal is possible with stronger evidence.
nextActions: Run the planned waitlist test of three offer-positioning variants with ten creators as stated in nextAction, and pre-register pass/fail criteria before launch.
Add at least one behavioral or outcome metric (e.g., offer completion, waitlist-to-action conversion) alongside click-through to strengthen the next decision.
Treat the automated-campaign generation feature as deferred to a later release rather than dropped, pending a second test that re-examines the speed assumption with a more committed user segment.
